note: Stub — future agent will generate questions from lesson content and quiz results

questions.question: What is the difference between git merge and git rebase?
questions.hint: Merge creates a merge commit; rebase rewrites history linearly.

questions.question: When should you use a feature branch instead of committing to main?
questions.hint: Any change that might break others — or that needs review.

questions.question: What is the difference between GET and POST in REST APIs?
questions.hint: GET retrieves data; POST creates or sends data to the server.

questions.question: What Python function parses a JSON string into a dictionary?
questions.hint: json.loads() for strings; json.load() for file objects.

questions.question: What is the geometric meaning of a dot product?
questions.hint: The projection of one vector onto another — related to the cosine of the angle between them.

questions.question: What does it mean for two vectors to be orthogonal?
questions.hint: Their dot product equals zero — they are perpendicular in n-dimensional space.

questions.question: Why is matrix multiplication NOT commutative — why does order matter?
questions.hint: Each matrix applies a geometric transformation; the order of transformations changes the outcome.

questions.question: What does an eigenvector tell you about a matrix transformation?
questions.hint: It is the direction that only gets scaled (not rotated) by the transformation.

questions.question: How does cosine similarity relate to dot products — and why does RAG use it?
questions.hint: Cosine similarity is the dot product of unit vectors — it measures direction, not magnitude.

questions.question: Explain conditional probability in plain English with one real example.
questions.hint: P(A|B) — the probability of A given that B has already happened. E.g. P(rain|cloudy).

questions.question: Why does the normal distribution appear so often in nature and in ML?
questions.hint: Central Limit Theorem — the sum of many independent random variables tends to a normal distribution.

questions.question: Walk through Bayes' theorem with a medical test example.
questions.hint: P(Disease|Positive) = P(Positive|Disease) × P(Disease) / P(Positive). Prior × likelihood / evidence.

questions.question: What is the difference between descriptive statistics and inferential statistics?
questions.hint: Descriptive summarizes data you have; inferential makes predictions about data you don't have.

questions.question: Why is NumPy so much faster than Python lists for numerical operations?
questions.hint: Vectorized operations, C implementation under the hood, and contiguous memory layout.

questions.question: What is Ollama and why does a local LLM matter for an AI engineer?
questions.hint: Run open-weight models locally — no API costs, privacy, and ability to experiment freely.
